findings.summary: Docstring bloat on trivial wrapper functions in logging_utils.py
evidence: log_substep has a 10-line docstring for a 2-line function that calls logger.info with an f-string
log_stage has a 12-line docstring with example output for a 4-line function
log_success has redundant emoji-selection logic that restates the function name
findings.suggestion: Reduce docstrings on trivial formatting helpers to a single-line description; remove example-output blocks from functions whose behavior is fully described by their name and signature.

findings.summary: Three thin module-level pass-through functions in health_check.py wrap a singleton with no added policy
evidence: quick_health_check() calls _get_checker().is_healthy() — one statement, no logic
get_health_status() calls _get_checker().get_health_status() — one statement, no logic
get_health_metrics() calls _get_checker().get_metrics() — one statement, no logic
findings.suggestion: Expose a module-level singleton directly or collapse into a single get_health() entry point, eliminating the three one-liner wrappers.

findings.summary: generate_pipeline_report unpacks ReportExtras field-by-field into locals that are immediately re-used only once
evidence: Lines 68-73 unpack all six fields of extras into identically-named locals
Each local is then used exactly once when constructing PipelineReport
Pattern: x = extras.x if extras is not None else None repeated six times reads as AI padding
findings.suggestion: Use extras or ReportExtras() pattern then reference fields directly in the PipelineReport constructor call.

findings.summary: logging_utils.py is a re-export facade that adds no policy — logic lives in four split modules
evidence: logging_utils imports from logging_helpers, logging_progress, logging_formatters, and logging_constants, then re-exports everything in __all__
The split was done for file size, not ownership — callers traverse an extra indirection hop with zero benefit
findings.suggestion: Either collapse the internal split modules back into logging_utils or promote them to explicit stable public packages with their own __all__.

findings.summary: ReportExtras is a single-use parameter bag that duplicates PipelineReport's optional fields
evidence: ReportExtras has six fields that mirror the optional fields of PipelineReport exactly
Its sole purpose is to bundle the six kwargs of generate_pipeline_report — never stored, mutated, or reused
findings.suggestion: Remove ReportExtras and accept the optional fields directly as keyword arguments on generate_pipeline_report.

findings.summary: logging_helpers.py uses a function-level import to dodge a circular dependency — no other infrastructure module does this
evidence: logging_helpers.py has 'from infrastructure.core.exceptions import TemplateError' inside the function body, unlike all sibling modules which import at top level
logging_utils.py __all__ re-exports EMOJIS, USE_EMOJIS — symbols that conceptually belong in their own modules
findings.suggestion: Move the TemplateError import to module level or consolidate logging_helpers into logging_utils.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/__init__.py uses try/except ImportError to set names to None — pattern absent from sibling __init__.py files
evidence: reporting/__init__.py: generate_all_dashboards assigned to None on ImportError
validation/__init__.py: all imports are unconditional, no None-fallback pattern
findings.suggestion: Move optional dashboard symbols out of __all__ or raise a clear ImportError at call time rather than silently setting names to None.

findings.summary: PerformanceMonitor.stop() raises BuildError but StagePerformanceTracker.end_stage() returns empty dict for same precondition
evidence: performance.py: 'raise BuildError("Monitor not started")' in PerformanceMonitor.stop()
performance.py: 'if self.start_time is None: return {}' in StagePerformanceTracker.end_stage() — same precondition, opposite strategy
performance_context uses bare finally block that calls stop() unconditionally — exception in stop() replaces original
findings.suggestion: Align the two tracker classes on a single error strategy. Guard stop() in performance_context so it does not run when the body raised.

findings.summary: performance.py houses two unrelated sub-domains despite explicit acknowledgment they should be split
evidence: Module docstring explicitly names two distinct sub-domains and anticipates a split into stage_monitor.py and function_profiler.py
PerformanceMonitor and CodeProfiler have no shared state and zero interdependency
findings.suggestion: Resolve the circular import root cause and split into stage_monitor.py and function_profiler.py as the module itself recommends.

findings.summary: generate_pipeline_report unpacks ReportExtras field-by-field into local variables then re-passes them — no-op abstraction
evidence: Six consecutive assignments extract every field of extras into local variables only to pass them directly to PipelineReport
The log-file side-effect modifies output_statistics in place, mixing data mutation with report construction inside a factory function
findings.suggestion: Pass extras fields directly to PipelineReport or let ReportExtras carry its own to_pipeline_report() conversion so the factory stays pure.

findings.summary: logging_utils.py re-exports 30+ items from five split submodules making it a wide aggregation facade with no logic
evidence: __all__ lists 30 symbols spanning formatters, progress utilities, ETA functions, spinner classes, constants
Pure re-export hub with no logic beyond get_logger/setup_logger
findings.suggestion: Shrink __all__ to the core logging API. Callers needing progress utilities should import from their respective submodules directly.

findings.summary: Pipeline executor abort-on-failure behavior has no cross-stage integration test
evidence: No test runs execute_core_pipeline with a mid-pipeline failure and verifies subsequent stages are not invoked
_execute_pipeline breaks on first failure — core correctness guarantee with no integration-level test
findings.suggestion: Add a test with one script that exits 1 and verify execute_core_pipeline returns only results up to and including the failing stage.

findings.summary: CodeProfiler and monitor_performance decorator have no tests — only psutil sub-domain is covered
evidence: test_performance.py imports only psutil-based classes — CodeProfiler, ProfilingMetrics, monitor_performance entirely absent
CodeProfiler.monitor() contains tracemalloc coordination logic that is non-trivial and completely untested
findings.suggestion: Add tests for CodeProfiler.monitor(), benchmark_function(), and the monitor_performance decorator.

findings.summary: logging_helpers.py has a function-level import creating an implicit circular dependency workaround at the boundary
evidence: logging_helpers.py imports TemplateError inside format_error_with_suggestions to avoid a circular import with logging_utils
logging_utils imports from logging_progress, which imports from logging_constants — the split creates a fan-in dependency hub at logging_utils with 5 upstream modules
The circular import workaround indicates a boundary direction problem: helpers should not depend on the aggregator
findings.suggestion: Move TemplateError import to module level by resolving the direction: exceptions should be a leaf module imported by helpers without creating a cycle back through logging_utils.

findings.summary: numpy and matplotlib are unconditional runtime deps but only needed in project-specific analysis scripts
evidence: numpy and matplotlib listed as core dependencies in pyproject.toml
Infrastructure code does not use numpy/matplotlib — they are only needed in projects/*/scripts/ analysis code
psutil, reportlab, plotly are already guarded with optional import patterns; numpy/matplotlib are not
findings.suggestion: Move numpy and matplotlib to an optional 'scientific' dependency group and guard infrastructure imports as done for psutil.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ contains 20+ files with mixed responsibilities not divided into subpackages
evidence: infrastructure/core/ contains logging_utils, logging_helpers, logging_progress, logging_formatters, logging_constants (5 logging files), performance, health_check, checkpoint, pipeline, config_loader, environment, exceptions (12+ files) all at the same level
The logging files would naturally group into a logging/ subpackage but remain flat
findings.suggestion: Consider moving the 5 logging_* files into infrastructure/core/logging/ subpackage to reduce cognitive load when navigating core/.

findings.summary: The logging module split into 5 sibling files blurs the top-level architecture — users cannot tell which is the public entry point
evidence: Five sibling files with 'logging_' prefix exist at the same level — newcomers cannot tell that logging_utils.py is the public entry point
The split was done for file-size reasons, not domain ownership — the architecture doesn't match a natural ownership boundary
findings.suggestion: Either consolidate the split files back into logging_utils.py (if cohesion is high) or move them into a logging/ subpackage with a clear __init__.py as the public API.

findings.summary: generate_pipeline_report mixes data construction, side-effect mutation, and optional field unpacking in one function body
evidence: Function unpacks ReportExtras, builds stage list, conditionally mutates output_statistics (adds log_file info), then constructs PipelineReport — three distinct concerns in one function
The log_file side-effect block (lines 87-94) modifies a dict passed in via extras before feeding it to the report object, creating a hidden mutation at the seam
findings.suggestion: Separate data preparation (including log_file enrichment) from report object construction. The factory function should receive already-complete data.

findings.summary: Several public functions in logging_utils.py still have verbose Args/Returns docstrings that duplicate type annotations
evidence: setup_logger has Args: name, level, log_file and Returns: sections that restate the type annotations already present in the signature
log_operation context manager has a 15-line docstring for a function whose behavior is fully described by name + type signature
findings.suggestion: Replace multi-section docstrings on well-typed functions with a single summary line. The type annotations are the contract; prose repetition adds noise.
